children.name: Chionis
children.description: The only Antarctic bird family without webbed feet, sheathbills are stout, pigeon-like shorebirds named for the horny sheath covering the base of their bill. They are opportunistic scavengers that follow penguin colonies and seal haul-outs, feeding on eggs, carrion, and any organic debris. Their white plumage provides camouflage against the snow and ice of their harsh sub-Antarctic and Antarctic home.

children.name: Chionis albus
children.description: The larger of the two sheathbill species, breeding on the Antarctic Peninsula and sub-Antarctic islands like South Georgia. It follows penguin colonies relentlessly, snatching eggs, chicks, and stolen krill with remarkable agility. Outside the breeding season it migrates north to the coasts of southern South America and the Falkland Islands.

children.name: Chionis minor
children.description: Restricted to the islands of the southern Indian Ocean: the Prince Edward, Crozet, Kerguelen and Heard Islands. It is distinguished from the Snowy Sheathbill by its darker face and smaller stature. Like its congener, it is a bold and resourceful scavenger around penguin and seal colonies.
